title: Georgia's 'Remotely from Georgia' Program: A Simple Visa Path

author.name: Isabelle Rossi
author.bio: Having lived and worked in over 30 countries, Isabelle is a leading voice on the digital nomad movement. She covers everything from visa strategies and travel hacking to maintaining well-being on the road.

reviewedBy.name: Dr. Alistair Finch
reviewedBy.bio: With a Ph.D. in Economics and over 15 years at a Big Four accounting firm, Alistair specializes in demystifying cross-border tax law for independent professionals. He focuses on risk mitigation and long-term financial planning.

key_takeaways: Calculate your true financial upside by modeling your home country's tax obligations, especially U.S. citizenship-based taxation, alongside Georgian setup and compliance costs, rather than relying solely on the 1% headline rate.
Actively mitigate the unlimited personal liability of Georgia's Individual Entrepreneur (IE) status by securing professional liability insurance and utilizing legally robust client contracts.

U.S. citizens must continue to file annual IRS returns and FBAR reports, strategically using the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE) to offset U.S. income tax, as Georgia's low tax rate provides minimal value via the Foreign Tax Credit (FTC).

Establish a non-negotiable monthly compliance rhythm by declaring your gross income to the Georgian Revenue Service by the 15th of each month to maintain your Small Business Status, even for months with zero income.
summary: While Georgia's 1% tax rate attracts global professionals, the surrounding online hype creates significant uncertainty about hidden legal and financial risks. This playbook provides a CEO-level framework for analyzing the true financial upside beyond the headlines, executing a compliant operational setup, and proactively managing critical risks like home-country tax obligations and personal liability. By following this structured approach, the reader can replace compliance anxiety with confidence, enabling them to make a defensible decision on whether Georgia is a profitable and sustainable base for their business.
ai_overview_answer:
To leverage Georgia's 1% tax rate, a professional must register as an Individual Entrepreneur (IE) and obtain 'Small Business Status'. This special status applies a 1% tax on annual business turnover up to GEL 500,000 (approximately $180,000 USD), with income exceeding that threshold taxed at 3%. The setup process involves securing a legal address, registering as an IE at a Public Service Hall, applying for the status with the Revenue Service, and opening a Georgian business bank account. Ongoing compliance requires a mandatory monthly tax declaration and payment by the 15th of each month.

For U.S. citizens, this does not eliminate IRS filing obligations; they must still file annual tax returns and report foreign bank accounts (FBAR). To mitigate double taxation, Americans typically use the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE) but remain liable for U.S. self-employment taxes. A critical consideration for all is that the IE structure carries unlimited personal liability, making professional liability insurance essential. Finally, while the official remote work program is suspended, citizens of over 95 countries can enter Georgia visa-free for one year, which serves as the primary entry mechanism.

itemListElement.name: Georgia's 'Remotely from Georgia' Program: A Simple Visa Path

@graph.name: How to Set Up Your Business in Georgia for the 1% Tax Rate
@graph.description: A step-by-step guide for global professionals to register as an Individual Entrepreneur in Georgia, secure Small Business Status for the 1% tax rate, and establish a compliant operational base.
@graph.headline: Georgia's 'Remotely from Georgia' Program: A Simple Visa Path

step.name: Secure a Legal Address
step.text: This is a mandatory requirement. You can use your rental address (with landlord permission), a property you own, or engage a service that provides a legal 'virtual' address.

step.name: Translate & Notarize Your Passport
step.text: A local notary bureau will create a notarized Georgian translation of your passport, the official document you’ll use for all subsequent steps.

step.name: Visit the Public Service Hall (House of Justice)
step.text: This is the central hub for all government registrations. With your notarized passport copy and legal address, you can file for IE status in person, often in less than a day for an expedited fee.

step.name: Register with the Revenue Service
step.text: Once your IE is approved, you will receive a tax identification number. You must then register on the Revenue Service's online portal and formally apply for Small Business Status. This status is what grants you the 1% tax rate.

step.name: Open Your Business Bank Account
step.text: With your registration documents and tax ID, you can now approach a Georgian bank to open your dedicated business account.

mainEntity.name: Is the 'Remotely from Georgia' program still active?

acceptedAnswer.text: The official online portal is suspended, but this has little practical impact. Citizens of over 95 countries (including the U.S., UK, and EU) can enter Georgia for a one-year visa-free stay. This policy has long been the primary, most straightforward mechanism for professionals to arrive and establish their operational base.

mainEntity.name: How does the 1% tax in Georgia work?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 1% rate is not automatic. It's granted to an 'Individual Entrepreneur' (IE) with 'Small Business Status' on annual turnover up to GEL 500,000 (approx. $180,000 USD). Maintaining this status requires a simple monthly tax declaration and payment to the Georgian Revenue Service.

mainEntity.name: Can a US citizen work remotely from Georgia?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. A U.S. citizen can work remotely in Georgia for up to one year visa-free. The critical point is that U.S. tax obligations remain. You must file an annual IRS tax return and report foreign bank accounts (FBAR), using tools like the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE) to mitigate double taxation. Filing is non-negotiable.

mainEntity.name: What are the income requirements to move to Georgia?

acceptedAnswer.text: For the common one-year visa-free entry, there is no strict income verification performed at the border. However, as a core principle of professional planning, you must be able to support yourself and should have bank statements or other proof of funds readily available in case an immigration official asks for evidence of self-sufficiency.

mainEntity.name: What happens if my business turnover exceeds the GEL 500,000 threshold?

acceptedAnswer.text: Once your annual turnover surpasses the GEL 500,000 mark, the tax rate on the income exceeding that threshold increases from 1% to 3%. You do not lose your status for the remainder of that calendar year. However, if you exceed the turnover cap for two consecutive years, your Small Business Status will be automatically revoked.

mainEntity.name: Do I need health insurance to work remotely from Georgia?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, this is a mandatory requirement. As of June 1, 2024, all visitors entering Georgia under the visa-free regime must have valid travel or international health insurance that covers them for their entire period of stay. You may be asked to present proof upon arrival. Beyond being a legal requirement, this is a non-negotiable part of your personal risk management strategy.

body.content: The CEO's Playbook for Georgia: Beyond the 1% Tax Hype

If you are a global professional, the online chatter about Georgia's 1% tax regime likely triggers two distinct feelings: excitement and a deep sense of unease. The excitement is understandable; retaining 99% of your revenue is a powerful incentive. The unease, however, is your professional experience talking. It’s the voice that recognizes headlines omit the fine print—and for a "Business-of-One," the fine print is where catastrophic risks hide.

The flood of generic "digital nomad" content presents a dangerously incomplete picture, treating a significant financial decision as a casual travel choice. This is not a vacation. It is a strategic relocation of your entire business entity, and it demands a proportional level of scrutiny.

A critical point of clarification: this guide is for the country of **Georgia**, a strategic hub in the Caucasus region at the intersection of Europe and Asia—not the U.S. state. The distinction is crucial for your planning and compliance.

This is not another travel blog cataloging the best cafes in Tbilisi. This is **The CEO's Playbook for Georgia**. It's a framework designed for the global professional who operates with a CEO mindset. Your concerns are not about finding the cheapest apartment; they are about legal exposure, banking stability, long-term viability, and flawless compliance. This playbook delivers that plan in three phases: a comprehensive due diligence framework to help you rigorously assess the opportunity, execute the operational setup, and proactively mitigate risk. Our goal is to replace compliance anxiety with a confident, actionable strategy, empowering you to decide if establishing a base in Georgia is a defensible and profitable move for your enterprise.
Phase 1: The Opportunity Analysis — Is Georgia a Defensible Business Base?

To determine if Georgia is a defensible move, your analysis must begin with a clear-eyed look at the facts on the ground, cutting through the internet noise. A CEO-level assessment requires scrutinizing the legal framework, the true financial upside, and the strategic trade-offs compared to other global hubs. This phase is about building a confident business case, free from hype.

First, let's clarify the entry mechanism. While the official "Remotely from Georgia" application portal has been suspended, this is largely a procedural point. For citizens of over 95 countries—including the US, UK, and all EU member states—Georgia offers a one-year, visa-free stay upon arrival. This policy is the *de facto* long-stay visa that most global professionals use, making the country one of the most accessible in the world for an extended remote work setup. This straightforward entry removes a significant layer of bureaucratic friction common in other jurisdictions.
Beyond the 1% Hype — Modeling Your True Financial Upside

The 1% tax rate is the headline, and for good reason. By registering as an Individual Entrepreneur and qualifying for "Small Business Status," you are taxed at just 1% on your annual turnover up to GEL 500,000 (approximately $180,000 USD). Should your turnover exceed that threshold, the rate on the excess income becomes 3%. But a headline is not a balance sheet. To understand your actual net gain, you must model the full financial picture:

items: **One-Time Setup Costs:** Factor in modest but necessary expenses for business registration, legal address services, and any professional consultation to ensure your specific business activities qualify.

**Recurring Compliance Costs:** While minimal, you must account for monthly tax filings and potentially a small retainer for a local accountant to ensure flawless compliance with the Georgian Revenue Service.

**Home Country Tax Obligations:** This is the most critical and often overlooked variable. Your tax situation in your country of citizenship does not disappear. Professional fees for a home-country accountant who understands expat tax law are a non-negotiable business expense.

body.content: U.S. Citizen Alert — Navigating the FEIE vs. FTC Crossroads

For American professionals, this last point is paramount. The United States' citizenship-based taxation system means you **must** continue to file with the IRS every year, regardless of where you live. The 1% Georgian tax does *not* negate this obligation. Your strategic decision lies in how you mitigate double taxation, primarily through two tools:

items: **Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE):** This allows you to exclude a significant portion of your foreign-earned income from U.S. federal income tax (over $126,000 for tax year 2024).
**Foreign Tax Credit (FTC):** This gives you a dollar-for-dollar credit for taxes you've already paid to a foreign government.

body.content: In a low-tax country like Georgia, the **FEIE is often the more powerful tool**. A 1% tax paid in Georgia provides only a tiny credit via the FTC, leaving a large portion of your income exposed to U.S. taxes. The FEIE, by contrast, can eliminate the U.S. tax liability on the excluded amount entirely. However, qualifying for the FEIE requires meeting strict physical presence or bona fide residence tests, a crucial planning component.
Benchmarking Georgia's Value Proposition
A strategic decision requires context. Benchmarking Georgia against other popular hubs reveals its unique value proposition.

rows: Moderate (D8 Digital Nomad Visa with income proof of ~€3,280/mo).
Moderate (Remote Work Visa with income proof of ~$3,500/mo).
Possible, but requires structured investment or other ties.
Clear 5-year path to permanent residency and citizenship.
Possible, but typically tied to significant investment or employment.

body.content: This analysis makes the trade-offs clear. While the UAE offers a zero-tax environment, it comes with a significantly higher cost of living and setup. Portugal provides a clear path to EU residency but with a substantially higher tax burden. Georgia's primary value proposition is its unparalleled combination of an extremely low tax rate, minimal bureaucracy for entry, and a very low cost of operations.
Phase 2: The Operational Plan — Your 90-Day Relocation Checklist

Once you've affirmed the strategic case for Georgia, the focus shifts from analysis to execution. A brilliant strategy is only as good as its implementation, and your first 90 days on the ground are about establishing a firm operational and legal foundation. This phase replaces ambiguity with action, ensuring your "Business-of-One" is compliant and functional from day one.
The 5-Step Playbook for Individual Entrepreneur (IE) Registration

Gaining the coveted 1% tax rate requires registering as an Individual Entrepreneur and securing "Small Business Status." The process in Georgia is remarkably streamlined and business-friendly. Here is the precise, actionable sequence:

items: **Step 1: Secure a Legal Address.** This is a mandatory requirement. You can use your rental address (with landlord permission), a property you own, or engage a service that provides a legal "virtual" address.
**Step 2: Translate & Notarize Your Passport.** A local notary bureau will create a notarized Georgian translation of your passport, the official document you’ll use for all subsequent steps.

**Step 3: Visit the Public Service Hall (House of Justice).** This is the central hub for all government registrations. With your notarized passport copy and legal address, you can file for IE status in person, often in less than a day for an expedited fee.

**Step 4: Register with the Revenue Service.** Once your IE is approved, you will receive a tax identification number. You must then register on the Revenue Service's online portal and formally apply for Small Business Status. This status is what grants you the 1% tax rate.
**Step 5: Open Your Business Bank Account.** With your registration documents and tax ID, you can now approach a Georgian bank to open your dedicated business account.

body.content: Opening Your Georgian Business Bank Account

Your business bank account is the engine of your operation. Georgia’s financial sector is modern and welcoming to foreign entrepreneurs. Major banks like **TBC Bank** and **Bank of Georgia** are the most common choices, offering robust online banking and English-speaking representatives.
To open your account, you will complete a standard Know Your Customer (KYC) process. Be prepared to provide:

items: Your passport and its notarized translation.
Your IE registration documents and tax ID.
A Georgian phone number.
Proof of your legal address.

body.content: The bank will also ask about the nature of your business, expected transaction volumes, and the source of your funds. Having clear, concise answers ready will expedite the process. Once established, you can easily transfer operating capital from abroad, as Georgian banks use the standard IBAN format.
Establishing Your Compliance Rhythm

Peace of mind comes from a simple, repeatable compliance routine. Your primary obligation is the **monthly tax declaration**. By the 15th of each month, you must log into the Revenue Service portal and declare your gross income from the previous month, paying the corresponding 1% tax. This must be done even if you had zero income.

Digital nomads often underestimate how complex their tax situation can become... A major mistake is failing to track which states or countries they spend time in. This can unintentionally create tax residency in multiple jurisdictions.
body.author: Jose A. Cruz, a Certified Public Accountant and founder of Cruz Tax Advisory

body.content: By establishing this non-negotiable monthly rhythm, you build the discipline needed to manage your obligations effectively. For U.S. professionals, this diligence is even more critical, as a common error is failing to properly account for self-employment taxes on income, even if that income is excluded from U.S. income tax by the FEIE.
Phase 3: The Risk Mitigation Strategy — Managing Your "What Ifs"

A professional strategy anticipates turbulence. This is where we move from the "how-to" of setup to the "what if" of long-term sustainability. Building a resilient Business-of-One means identifying potential points of failure—legal, financial, and logistical—and proactively creating a playbook to manage them. This isn't pessimism; it's about securing the freedom you've worked to create.
Ring-Fencing Your Risk: The Legal Liability of an IE

The Individual Entrepreneur (IE) status is fast and tax-efficient, but it comes with a significant trade-off: **unlimited personal liability**. The IE structure does not create a separate legal entity from you, the owner. If your business incurs debts or faces a lawsuit, your personal assets—savings, property, and other investments—could be at risk. This stands in stark contrast to a Limited Liability Company (LLC), where an owner's liability is generally limited to their investment.
To manage this exposure while operating as an IE in Georgia:

items: **Professional Liability Insurance:** This is non-negotiable. Also known as Errors & Omissions (E&O) insurance, it protects your service-based business from claims of negligence.
**Rock-Solid Contracts:** Work with a lawyer to develop strong client contracts that clearly define the scope of work, deliverables, and limitations of liability.

**Separate Finances:** Maintain a dedicated business bank account and never co-mingle personal and business funds. This discipline is vital for both financial clarity and demonstrating professionalism.

body.content: The U.S. Compliance Backstop: FBAR and IRS Reporting
For American professionals, the greatest source of anxiety is often U.S. tax compliance. Let's demystify your two most important obligations while operating from Georgia.

First is the **Report of Foreign Bank and Financial Accounts (FBAR)**, or FinCEN Form 114. This is a report to the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN). If the combined total of all your foreign financial accounts exceeds **$10,000 at any point during the calendar year**, you must file an FBAR. This is an aggregate amount; if one account briefly hits $11,000 for a single day, the reporting requirement is triggered for all accounts.

Second is your **annual U.S. tax return**. Your Georgian IE income is reported on Schedule C (Profit or Loss from Business). You will then use Form 2555 to claim the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE) to offset your U.S. income tax liability. Remember: this does *not* eliminate your need to pay U.S. self-employment taxes (Social Security and Medicare) on that income. Diligent reporting is your best defense against costly penalties.
The Exit Strategy: A Playbook for a Clean Departure

Every strategic plan needs an exit clause. Whether you decide to move, return home, or change your business structure, a clean departure from the Georgian system is essential. An unplanned exit can create lingering tax and legal headaches.
Despite officially reported positive economic indicators, the business environment in Georgia is rapidly deteriorating.
body.author: Larisa Pataraia, Managing Partner of consulting firm Start-Business Solutions

body.content: This perspective underscores the importance of having a clear exit plan. Economic and political climates can shift, and a professional is always prepared to adapt.
Your exit playbook should be a simple checklist:

items: **Settle All Tax Obligations:** Ensure all monthly declarations are filed and all taxes are paid. Have a professional conduct a final audit to confirm no outstanding liabilities.
**Cancel Small Business Status:** This is a distinct step from closing your IE and must be done with the Revenue Service.
**Close Your Business Bank Account:** Once all client payments are received and final taxes are paid, formally close your Georgian business bank account.

**De-register as an Individual Entrepreneur:** The final step is to file an application at the Public Service Hall to terminate your IE status. This can be done in a single business day, even remotely with a power of attorney.

body.content: By planning your exit with the same diligence as your entry, you ensure your time in Georgia is a closed, compliant, and successful chapter in your professional story.
Frequently Asked Questions

Executing a professional relocation, even with a clear playbook, naturally leaves specific, tactical questions. This is the final layer of your due diligence—clearing up the ambiguities so you can operate with complete confidence.

items:
**Is the 'Remotely from Georgia' program still active?**
The official online portal is suspended, but this has little practical impact. Citizens of over 95 countries (including the U.S., UK, and EU) can enter Georgia for a **one-year visa-free stay**. This policy has long been the primary, most straightforward mechanism for professionals to arrive and establish their operational base.

**How does the 1% tax in Georgia work?**
The 1% rate is not automatic. It's granted to an **'Individual Entrepreneur' (IE)** with **'Small Business Status'** on annual turnover up to GEL 500,000 (approx. $180,000 USD). Maintaining this status requires a simple monthly tax declaration and payment to the Georgian Revenue Service.

**Can a US citizen work remotely from Georgia?**
Yes. A U.S. citizen can work remotely in Georgia for up to one year visa-free. The critical point is that **U.S. tax obligations remain**. You must file an annual IRS tax return and report foreign bank accounts (FBAR), using tools like the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE) to mitigate double taxation. Filing is non-negotiable.

**What are the income requirements to move to Georgia?**
For the common one-year visa-free entry, there is **no strict income verification** performed at the border. However, as a core principle of professional planning, you must be able to support yourself and should have bank statements or other proof of funds readily available in case an immigration official asks for evidence of self-sufficiency.

**What happens if my business turnover exceeds the GEL 500,000 threshold?**
Once your annual turnover surpasses the GEL 500,000 mark, the tax rate on the income exceeding that threshold increases from 1% to **3%**. You do not lose your status for the remainder of that calendar year. However, if you exceed the turnover cap for two consecutive years, your Small Business Status will be automatically revoked.

**Do I need health insurance to work remotely from Georgia?**
Yes, this is a mandatory requirement. As of June 1, 2024, all visitors entering Georgia under the visa-free regime must have valid travel or international health insurance that covers them for their entire period of stay. You may be asked to present proof upon arrival. Beyond being a legal requirement, this is a non-negotiable part of your personal risk management strategy.

body.content: Conclusion: Your Decision Framework

With these tactical questions addressed, you can elevate your perspective from the granular details of compliance back to the overarching strategic decision. Relocating your business to Georgia is not a travel choice spurred by online hype; it is a significant strategic pivot that demands professional-grade due diligence. It requires you to think less like a tourist and more like the CEO of your own global enterprise.

Throughout this playbook, we have systematically dismantled the decision into three core pillars: analyzing the opportunity, creating an operational plan, and mitigating risk. This structured approach is what separates a sustainable business strategy from a precarious gamble. It transforms uncertainty and compliance anxiety into a predictable, controllable system.
To crystallize your final decision, consider how this process has equipped you to answer the right questions:

rows: Does the financial upside withstand scrutiny after accounting for all compliance costs and home-country tax laws?
Move from headline numbers to a defensible Profit & Loss projection.
Do I have a clear, step-by-step process for establishing legal status, banking, and a consistent compliance rhythm?
Replace ambiguity with an actionable 90-day execution checklist.
Have I identified and ring-fenced my legal, financial, and personal liabilities, including a clear exit strategy?
Convert "what-if" anxieties into a concrete risk management protocol.

body.content: Ultimately, the flood of online content prompts a simple, superficial question: "Is Georgia a cheap place to live?" That was never the right question for you. A professional armed with this framework asks a more precise and powerful question: **"Is Georgia a sound, tax-optimized, and legally defensible operational base for my global business?"**
You now possess the strategic tools to answer that question with unwavering confidence.
